question: Considering only neutral messages posted in the last two days of July 2024 on chat "UNITED24Media Chat" talking about NATO, who has been offered the position of head of the command based in Wiesbaden?
relevant_doc:
Curtis Buzzard has been offered the position of head of the command based in Wiesbaden, Germany, which is responsible for providing military aid to Ukraine, according to the official publication of the US Department of Defense, Stars and Stripes.

If his candidacy is approved by the Senate, Buzzard will command several hundred military personnel who are part of the Security Assistance Group of Ukraine (SAG-U). This group is stationed at the headquarters of the US Army in Europe and Africa in Wiesbaden.

Buzzard, a graduate of the United States Military Academy, currently serves as commander of the United States Army Maneuver Center of Excellence at Fort Moore, Georgia. He will arrive in Wiesbaden during a transitional period for the aid mission to Ukraine.

At the NATO summit in Washington, Alliance countries agreed that the Security Assistance Group of Ukraine, led by the United States since 2022, will come under the bloc's control. The group is currently led by US Army Lt. Gen. Antonio Aguto Jr. In the future, NATO plans to take over the coordination and provision of most international security assistance. The mission, to be named "NATO — Security Assistance and Training for Ukraine," will be based in Wiesbaden and involve about 700 troops.

The new command is expected to be formed in a few weeks. The US Ground Forces in Europe and Africa have previously stated that SAG-U will continue to function. NATO's military headquarters did not immediately specify whether Buzzard would lead a separate alliance headquarters in Wiesbaden on a part-time basis.

question: Considering only neutral messages posted in the last two days of July 2024 on chat "Ukraine Report Chat" talking about Ukraine-Russia, which country refused to transfer income from Russia's frozen assets to Ukraine?
relevant_doc:
Switzerland refused to transfer income from Russia's frozen assets to Ukraine.

Switzerland will not follow the example of the European Union and will not transfer income from Russia's frozen assets to Ukraine, the State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (Seco, a division of the Swiss Ministry of Economy) told Keystone-SDA.

The department stressed that, according to the country's legislation and international obligations, Switzerland cannot receive "any extraordinary income in connection with the funds of the Russian Central Bank."

As of April 2024, the total value of the Central Bank of Russia's assets frozen in Switzerland amounted to just over 7 billion Swiss francs ($7.9 billion). At the same time, as noted in Seco, these funds do not generate "any special income."

In total, about $280 billion worth of Russian assets have been frozen in the G7 countries and the EU, as well as in Australia since the start of the war in Ukraine, most of which are at the disposal of the Belgian Euroclear group. In 2023, they generated €4.4 billion in net profit, and in the first quarter of this year, they generated another €1.6 billion. According to the Financial Times, by 2027, total profits could reach 20 billion euros.

Earlier, the EU countries agreed to transfer income from Russian assets to Kyiv. The European Commission noted that these revenues "are not sovereign assets," so the mechanism for their transfer to Ukraine does not violate property rights.

"People often write to me – how are you, dude?

Morning. I take my child to the kindergarten. Halfway there, an air raid siren starts; my son already knows this sound well, so he says – Dad, it's an alarm. Are rockets coming again? We turn back, and near the house, we hear an explosion and instinctively crouch. I already know that ballistic missiles fly so fast after the alarm. We run into the house. In the corridor, behind two walls from the street, we sit and listen; another explosion. The walls in the house vibrate, doors shake. An hour later, the alarm ends. I take my child to the kindergarten again. Two hours later, when the children are put to sleep for a nap in the kindergarten, the alarm sounds again. We receive a notification that MiG-31 fighters have taken off in Russia; we already know that these planes can launch 'Kinzhal' hypersonic missiles. At that moment, the children in the kindergarten are woken up, and together with schoolchildren, they are quickly taken to the basement – a bomb shelter. About two hundred children will have to sit there for about two hours, all together in a cramped space, waiting for the end of the alarm.

After two hours, the alarm is over, the missiles were not launched, the planes returned. Russians often do this, knowing that during the alarm everyone will run to shelters; these are their standard methods of intimidation. Evening, alarm again. When it gets dark, Russians launch drones, equipped with explosives, their number can vary from 20 to 80. They fly at a low altitude and make a loud, rattling sound, similar to the sound of a motorcycle. After some time, we hear the shooting of anti-aircraft guns trying to shoot down targets. In the sky, from all sides, the light of searchlights is visible, with which moving objects are found in the dark. Very close, we hear a drone flying somewhere in the area of ​​the house. A few minutes later, another one. The shooting continues, then a deafening explosion, the lights flicker, the walls shake.

This continues for two to three hours.
After some time, we lay the bed right there in the corridor, separated from the street by two thick walls, it’s at least some chance to stay alive if fragments of a shot down missile or drone hit the house. We understand that in case of a direct hit, the chances of survival are not great.
Night. Alarm, we sleep, don’t hear the notification. We are awakened by explosions. They sound even louder at night. We read messages that Russians fired many missiles. For several hours, we periodically hear a roar, closer or further. Doors and windows periodically shake. In the morning, the all-clear is given. Later in the official report, we read that a total of about 110 missiles and forty drones were fired, unfortunately, some of them could not be shot down. There are destructions, dead and wounded. Among the objects that suffered – shopping centers, hospitals, residential buildings, etc.

This happens almost every day, a different number of missiles or drones, Russians constantly launch them at all cities of Ukraine.
And so for two years.
Now you understand the essence of my answer – we are alive.
